title: Cat Dried Blood in Ear Flap: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Notice dried blood on your cat's ear? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
What Is Dried Blood on a Cat's Ear Flap?

Finding dried blood on your cat’s ear flap, or pinna, usually appears as small dark crusts or reddish-brown scabs. This is often the result of minor trauma to the skin's surface. Common causes include self-trauma from scratching due to allergies or ear mites, small nicks from outdoor encounters, or reactions to insect bites. While it looks alarming, it is frequently a localized skin issue rather than an internal injury.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for dried blood on the ear flap is considered Low. If your cat is behaving normally, eating well, and the bleeding has already stopped, you can typically monitor them at home and schedule a routine veterinary appointment. However, if you notice the ear flap becoming thick and pillow-like (an aural hematoma), or if your cat is showing signs of extreme pain or loss of balance, you should seek professional advice more quickly.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, high-resolution photo of the affected area is one of the most helpful things you can do for your vet. A photo allows a professional to see the exact pattern of the blood, the condition of the surrounding skin, and whether there is any discharge coming from the ear canal itself. This visual information helps determine if the issue is a simple surface scratch or a symptom of a deeper infection, ensuring your cat gets the specific treatment they need.

faqs.question: Can I clean the dried blood off my cat's ear?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe the outer ear flap with a soft, damp cloth and warm water, but avoid using hydrogen peroxide or sticking anything into the ear canal, as this can cause further irritation.

faqs.question: Does dried blood mean my cat has ear mites?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily, but it is a common sign. Mites cause intense itching; if a cat scratches their ear too hard, they may break the skin on the flap, leading to minor bleeding and scabbing.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my cat's ear?
faqs.answer: You should seek care if the ear is persistently bleeding, if there is a foul odor, if the ear flap appears swollen, or if your cat is constantly shaking their head.
